one_line_takeaway: By training LLMs to emit an explicit resource-review / identity-resolution / decision reasoning trajectory before answering, jointly optimized with the downstream task via a single SFT objective, Chain-of-Authorization drives unauthorized-access accuracy to near-zero and adversarial jailbreak success rates to near-zero on WMDP while retaining SFT-level task utility, substantially outperforming prompt-guided (sudoLM) and structural-isolation (Permissioned LLM) baselines across three backbones and five datasets.
key_finding: On Table 1 (accuracy, %), CoA is close to the SFT utility ceiling across backbones and datasets, e.g. Mobile-Actions accuracy 94.53-96.09% across Qwen3-1.7B/Llama-3.1-8B/Mistral-7B (SFT: 95.16/95.31/95.78%), and on Mistral-7B CoA even exceeds SFT on WMDP (68.98% vs 67.48%, +1.50) and Mobile-Actions (96.09% vs 95.78%, +0.31). Competing baselines lose far more utility relative to SFT: PermLM drops 54.69 points on SQuAD accuracy for Qwen3 (31.95% vs 86.64% SFT) because it can only apply context filtering there, and sudoLM collapses catastrophically on Llama-3.1-8B (e.g., MMLU 14.42% vs 69.49% SFT, a 55.07-point drop). On unauthorized-state accuracy (Figure 2), CoA reduces accuracy to near 0 across all five datasets in both public and mismatch states (the 'Optimal Zone'), while Base and plain SFT retain high accuracy (i.e., they leak controlled information) and PermLM and sudoLM only partially suppress it. On rejection rate, CoA reaches near-100% in both public and mismatch states across all datasets/backbones, versus near-0% rejection for Base/SFT. Under adversarial attack (Table 2, WMDP, ASR%, mismatch/public format), CoA achieves e.g. style-injection ASR of 0.14/9.12% on Qwen3-1.7B and 0.14/0.14% on Llama3-8B versus sudoLM's 100.00/99.86% and 51.43/7.89% respectively; the paper highlights CoA cutting Llama-3-8B-Instruct style-injection ASR from 52.52% (stated for sudoLM in text) to 0.14%, and CoA achieves 0.00% ASR against GPT-4o-driven persuasive attacks on Llama-3.1-8B and Mistral-7B in the public state. t-SNE visualization shows match/mismatch/public representations overlap at the prompt's end for both the base model and the CoA model, but form three distinct non-overlapping clusters at the end of the CoA reasoning trajectory -- interpreted as CoA separating authorization states in representation space only after reasoning, not from the permission-label tokens alone. The causal-intervention experiment shows injecting a contradictory judgment at any CoA stage (system prompt, resource review, identity resolution, or decision) flips rejection rate from 0% to 100% even under an otherwise valid permission match, which the authors use as evidence the model's behavior is driven by the reasoning trajectory's internal logical state rather than superficial keyword matching. The learning-rate ablation (Fig. 5) shows learning rate must exceed 1e-4 for the model to learn to distinguish authorization states at all -- at 1e-5 the model performs near-random regardless of epoch, while at 5e-4 it reaches 69.12% match-state accuracy by epoch 3 alongside near-100% rejection in mismatch/public states.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Chain-of-Authorization (CoA), a training/reasoning paradigm that internalizes access-control (authorization) decisions into an LLM's own generation process rather than enforcing them via an external filter. CoA does two things: (1) restructures the model input by injecting permission labels for the user (C_u), tools (C_g), and retrieved external context (C_e) into the system prompt / context, and (2) forces the model, before producing a substantive answer Y, to emit a structured 'authorization reasoning trajectory' T = (T_res, T_id, T_dec) consisting of a resource-review stage (what permission does the requested knowledge/context/tool require), an identity-resolution stage (what permission does this user actually hold), and a decision stage (match/mismatch/public -> allow or reject). The model is supervised-fine-tuned (LoRA) with a single joint negative-log-likelihood objective over (T, Y) on synthesized data covering three authorization states -- matched, mismatched, and public/unauthenticated -- so the authorization decision and the downstream task output are learned jointly rather than via a separate classifier or auxiliary loss.
threat_model: The paper targets LLMs acting as the 'cognitive core' of agentic AI systems (tool use, RAG, enterprise data access) that must enforce access control over heterogeneous information (internal parametric knowledge, externally retrieved context, and callable tools) held at different permission levels for different users. The specific risk framed is the 'Confused Deputy' problem: an agent with high-privilege access is misled -- e.g., via instructions embedded in a low-privilege public webpage/document (indirect prompt injection, citing Abdelnabi et al. and Liu et al.) -- into using its privileges on behalf of an unauthorized requester, causing sensitive data leakage or unauthorized tool/command execution. The paper explicitly distinguishes two failure/attack surfaces it defends against: (a) public/unauthenticated users with zero permission labels, and (b) users who hold some but not all of the permissions a request requires ('permission mismatch'). It also evaluates against active adversarial attackers attempting to elicit unauthorized content: manual jailbreak prompts (prefix injection, style injection, from EasyJailbreak), LLM-generated persuasion attacks (PAP, 5 strategies generated by GPT-4o), and an automated iterative jailbreak search (PAIR, using DeepSeek-V3.2 as attacker and GPT-4.1-mini as judge).
limitations: The paper does not state a distinct, separately labeled limitations section; the following are limitations evident from its own reported results and design. (1) The 'External Gateway'/SFT+Extra baseline is marked '/' (training failure or infeasible) on SQuAD and COVID-QA in Table 1, so no head-to-head comparison against an external-classifier gateway exists for the external-context tasks. (2) CoA still shows non-trivial utility loss relative to SFT on some tasks (e.g., MMLU accuracy down 3.10-10.32 points and SQuAD down 1.96-6.52 points across the three backbones), i.e., internalizing authorization is not fully free of utility cost, especially on Mistral-7B. (3) Rejection is operationalized purely as the response text beginning with the literal string 'Sorry,' a coarse detection heuristic that the authors themselves note is used 'for statistical convenience' -- this could undercount refusals phrased differently or overcount unrelated uses of 'Sorry.' (4) The robustness evaluation (Table 2, adversarial attacks) is conducted only on WMDP, not replicated across MMLU/SQuAD/COVID-QA/Mobile-Actions, so generalization of adversarial robustness to other permission topologies (e.g., the much larger 1234-label COVID-QA or the tool-calling setting) is untested. (5) All permission structures are synthetic/author-constructed relabelings of existing QA/tool datasets (document titles, subject tags, object-type/read-write dimensions) rather than real deployed enterprise access-control policies, and permission granularity is coarse (WMDP has only 3 labels; Mobile-Actions only 5-7) relative to real multi-tenant systems with deep permission hierarchies. (6) The approach requires retraining/fine-tuning the model whenever the permission-label vocabulary or policy changes (embedding layer must be expanded for new labels), unlike prompt-based or purely external gateway approaches, which the paper does not quantify the operational cost of.

datasets_used: WMDP (Weapons of Mass Destruction Proxy) -- 3 permission labels (bio/chem/cyber), 2936 train / 732 test
MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) -- 57 permission labels (subject areas), 11233 train / 2809 test
SQuAD -- 100 permission labels (document titles), 2262 train / 2036 test
COVID-QA (subset via RagBench, original from Moller et al.) -- 1234 permission labels (document titles), 1252 train / 155 test
Mobile-Actions (Google) -- 5-7 permission labels (object type x read/write), 5794 train / 640 test
quantitative_results: Table 1 accuracy: CoA on Mobile-Actions 94.53-96.09% across Qwen3-1.7B/Llama-3.1-8B/Mistral-7B vs SFT 95.16/95.31/95.78%
Table 1: CoA exceeds SFT on Mistral-7B WMDP (68.98% vs 67.48%, +1.50) and Mobile-Actions (96.09% vs 95.78%, +0.31)
Table 1: PermLM SQuAD accuracy on Qwen3-1.7B collapses to 31.95% vs SFT 86.64% (-54.69 points)
Table 1: sudoLM MMLU accuracy on Llama-3.1-8B collapses to 14.42% vs SFT 69.49% (-55.07 points)

Figure 2: CoA reduces unauthorized-state accuracy to near 0 across all 5 datasets/3 backbones (public and mismatch states) and achieves near-100% rejection rate in both states; Base/SFT show near-0% rejection rate

Table 2 ASR on WMDP, style injection: CoA 0.14/9.12% (mismatch/public) on Qwen3-1.7B, 0.14/0.14% on Llama3-8B, 0.14/0.00% on Mistral-7B, vs sudoLM 100.00/99.86%, 51.43/7.89%, 98.10/66.12% respectively
Text: CoA reduces Llama-3-8B-Instruct style-injection ASR from 52.52% to 0.14%
Table 2: CoA achieves 0.00% ASR under GPT-4o-driven PAP persuasive attacks on Llama-3.1-8B and Mistral-7B in public state
Figure 5 ablation (Llama3-8B/WMDP): at learning rate 5e-4, match-state accuracy reaches 69.12% by epoch 3; at learning rate 1e-5 the model performs near-random regardless of epoch
Causal intervention (Qwen3-1.7B/WMDP): injecting a contradictory judgment at any CoA stage flips rejection rate from 0% to 100%
baselines_compared: SFT (direct supervised fine-tuning, no authorization mechanism)
SFT+Extra / External Gateway (RoBERTa-base classifier filters context before the fine-tuned LLM)
Permissioned LLM / PermLM (Jayaraman et al. 2025 -- sub-models per permission or context filtering)
sudoLM (Liu et al. 2025 -- prompt-guided access control via system-prompt permission labels only)
